title: Bundle Size
intro: Your app's JavaScript bundle keeps growing. Every new feature adds another library, and nobody's checking the cost. Let's use command-line tools to measure your bundle, find the heavy dependencies, and trim the fat.

teach.title: Bundle Size = Suitcase Weight
teach.body: Airlines have a weight limit for luggage. If your suitcase is over the limit, you pay extra and everything takes longer. Your JavaScript bundle is like that suitcase -- the heavier it is, the longer users wait. And just like packing, the solution is removing things you don't really need, not buying a bigger suitcase.

teach.title: Why Bundle Size Matters
teach.body:
Every kilobyte of JavaScript has a double cost:

1. **Download time** -- Users must download the file before anything happens
2. **Parse time** -- The browser must read and compile the JavaScript before it can run

On a slow 3G connection, 1MB of JavaScript takes about 10 seconds to download AND another few seconds to parse. That's why bundle size directly translates to load time.

teach.title: Import Only What You Need
teach.annotation: The first import pulls in 500+ lodash functions. The second import only includes sortBy and its dependencies. This is called tree-shaking, and it can cut library sizes by 90%.

teach.body: Every kilobyte of JavaScript costs download time AND parse time. Import only what you need from libraries, regularly audit your dependencies, and set a performance budget to catch bloat early.

steps.instruction: Let's check how many dependencies your project has. This gives you a sense of how bloated things might be.
steps.output:
my-app@1.0.0
+-- react@18.2.0
+-- react-dom@18.2.0
+-- axios@1.4.0
+-- lodash@4.17.21
+-- moment@2.29.4
+-- chart.js@4.3.0
+-- react-icons@4.10.1
+-- @mui/material@5.14.0
+-- date-fns@2.30.0
+-- uuid@9.0.0
`-- 14 more dependencies
steps.hint: Use 'npm ls --depth=0' to list your direct dependencies without showing their sub-dependencies.

steps.instruction: You have both moment AND date-fns -- two date libraries doing the same thing! Let's check which packages are the heaviest. Use npm to check the installed size of moment.
steps.hint: Use 'du -sh' to check the disk size of the moment package in node_modules.

steps.instruction: 4.2 MB for moment! Most of that is locale files for every language. date-fns is already installed and does the same thing but is tree-shakeable. Let's remove moment.
steps.hint: Use 'npm uninstall' followed by the package name to remove it.

steps.instruction: Now let's check if any of your dependencies have known smaller alternatives. Use the bundlephobia-inspired size checker.
steps.output:
Package sizes (minified + gzipped):
  lodash     71.5 KB  (consider: lodash-es for tree-shaking)
  axios      13.6 KB  (consider: native fetch API - 0 KB)
  uuid        2.4 KB  (consider: crypto.randomUUID() - 0 KB)
steps.hint: Use 'npx package-size' to check the gzipped size of packages and see if smaller alternatives exist.

steps.instruction: Good insights! axios can be replaced with the built-in fetch API, and uuid can use the built-in crypto.randomUUID(). Let's remove axios since fetch is built into every modern browser.
steps.hint: Use 'npm uninstall axios' to remove it. You'll replace axios calls with the native fetch API.

exercise.completionMessage: You've removed moment (4.2MB / ~71KB gzipped) and axios (13.6KB gzipped) by using built-in alternatives that cost zero bytes. Combined with switching to lodash-es for tree-shaking, you could cut your bundle by over 100KB gzipped. Always question whether you need a library for something the platform provides natively.
